evidences.title: Central American Electric-Knife Fights Championship and ...
evidences.content: ​Born in the biohacking underground, electric knife fighting is a point-style combat sport with high voltage sparring knives. Fighters must score points and avoid pain to win cash, prizes, and glory. For over a decade, this heart-pumping, high-adrenaline sport has grown in popularity, attracting fighters internationally. Matches have been held everywhere from high desert biohacker events, San Francisco raves, Caribbean beaches, bars, speakeasies, and the jungles of Central America. ​Now, with the blessing of Sindicato de Las Sombras, electric knife fighting is Infinita City’s official sport and the blockbuster highlight of The Infinite Games.​ ​ ​Enjoy the best techno, house, minimal techno, and electro music from DJs: ​Supersonico ​Diego Lucio ​Adrian Alessandro [...] ​Supersonico ​Diego Lucio ​Adrian Alessandro ​Elias (Fractal Music) ​DFL (Fractal Music resident DJ) ​The Hanged man (USA) ​and more! # ​🧩 How To Join ​General Admission (audience) ​Tickets: $10 (includes 3 drinks) ​OR ​FIGHTERS register here for free (limited space available): ​ # ​🛠 Format ​​The top qualifiers square off in knockout rounds: Quarterfinals, Semifinals, and Finals. ​Fighters will need to score 2 po...

Shop Now #### World Knife Throwing Championship ### Tournament Format #### Finding the Best of the Best The World Knife Throwing Championship may vary in format but is most commonly a televised double-elmination tournament. [...] Competitors will be put into two sets of brackets, the Winner's bracket (A Bracket) and the Underdog bracket (B Brackets). The A bracket is conducted in the same manner as a single-elimination tournament, except that the losers of each. ### The world Knife Throwing Championship Finals #### There can only be one winner The final World Knife Throwing Championship round is between the winner of the A bracket and the winner of the B bracket consisting of the best of 3 matches. evidences.title: The Rise of Competitive Axe and Knife Throwing — The Cut Axe Throwing
evidences.content: Highlights of WATL include: League Structure: WATL offers a season-long competitive framework where individual athletes and teams accumulate points across multiple events in their attempt to qualify for the World Axe and Knife Throwing Championships (WAKTC), adding an extra layer of strategy and long-term engagement. Dynamic Competition Format: With tournaments often hosted in popular axe-throwing venues across the U.S., WATL blends the excitement of live events with the competitive rigor of organized sports. Community Focus: WATL’s events are known for their festive, inclusive vibe. By integrating social elements into competitions, the league has helped axe throwing transition from a niche activity into a mainstream recreational sport. [...] Both the IATF and WATL have contributed significantly to transforming axe and knife throwing from a casual pastime into a well-respected competitive arena. Despite differences in structure—one emphasizing standardized international rules and the other championing a dynamic league format—each organization plays a critical role in growing the sport and ensuring that competitors have a safe, fair, and exciting environment in which to excel. ##...

evidences.title: The History of the Mystery Game – PolarBair
evidences.content: 5: Have a specific plan for how things will run. Plan out which games go where, what games you can run simultaneously with the available setups, and how to transition between setups quickly. 6: The more likely a game is to be unfair or gimmick a player out, the deeper into winners bracket it should be. This assumes a double elimination bracket, which is fairly standard in events. Obviously someone has to lose each match, but getting 0-2’d out of the tournament because someone knows how to do instant kills in the first Guilty Gear game is a pretty bad place to be compared to feeling like you got to at least play some footsies. [...] While we’re on that subject, Midwest Championships is a legendary tournament series itself. The earliest records come from 2000, when it was known as “Street Fighter Midwest Championships”, but tweets from DreamTR state it celebrated its 20th anniversary in June of 2013. According to Guardian, it was in Chicago for seven years and then moved to Omaha for a couple years in 03, then moved back to Chicago one or two years later. The important thing here is that MWC 2006 is the earliest mention of the Mystery Game Tournament we can find. This is thanks to...
